coredata.dc:title: Microwave-assisted fast preparation of functionalized carbon dots: The impact of ionic liquid precursor on corrosion inhibition

coredata.dc:description: In this study, functionalized carbon dots were synthesized in one step from L-histidine and imidazolyl ionic liquids using an ionic liquid-assisted microwave method as green corrosion inhibitors for steel in a 1 M HCl solution. Different ionic liquid anions (CF3COO- and HSO4 -) were introduced in the preparation of the carbon dots, and their microstructures and surface properties were characterized. Notably, the corrosion inhibition efficiency of the prepared CDs (assisted by CF3COO-) reached 94.2% at 5 mg‧dm−3. The corrosion inhibition behavior of CDs on mild steel in 1 M HCl was investigated through DFT calculations. Our investigation revealed that the anions of the selected ionic liquids have a certain influence on the structure of the carbon dots, which correlates with the performance of the carbon dot corrosion inhibitor. Therefore, this study provides guidance for the preparation of functionalized carbon dot corrosion inhibitors using a one-step microwave method and the selection of ionic liquids (as solvents, modifiers, and mediums for microwave conversion).

Full XPS spectra (a), C 1 s (b), N 1 s (c) and O 1 s (d) of as-prepared CDs1 and CDs2.
Fluorescence Spectroscopy of CDs (a) CDs1; (b) CDs2 at different excited wavelengths.
TEM microscopy images and size distributions of CDs1 (a1, a2) and CDs2 (b1, b2).
Nyquist plots and Bode plots for 45# steel measured in 1 M HCl solution both in the absence and presence of varying concentrations of (a, c) CDs1 and (b, d) CDs2.
Potentiodynamic polarization plots for carbon steel (45#) in 1 M HCl solution uninhibited and inhibited with different concentrations of the CDs. (a) CDs1, (b) CDs2.
Nyquist plots of 45# steel in 1 M HCl solution containing 20 ppm CDs1 at 298 K to 313 K.
Corrosion rate of steel in 1 M HCl solution without and with 20 ppm CDs of various immersion time.
SEM images taken from the 45# steel surfaces in 1 M HCl solution for 2 h (a) fresh polished steel surface, (b) without CDs; and present CDs1, (d) CDs2.
3D AFM images of the steel surface with (a) blank solution, (b) 20 ppm CDs1 and (c) 20 ppm CDs2.
XPS spectra of the steel surface inhibited by 20 ppm CDs1 in 1 M HCl solution (a-c) C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s, respectively.
Langmuir adsorption isotherms of 45# steel surface in (a) CDs1 and (b) CDs2.
Arrhenius (a) and Transition (b) plots for CDs1.
Structure, HOMO, LUMO and ESP potential of CDs1 and CDs2.
Equilibrium configuration for the adsorption of CDs1 molecule (a) side views, (b) top views and CDs2 molecule (c) side views, (d) top views on the Fe (1 1 0) surface.
Protective mechanisms of 45# steel in 1 M HCl solution.
Electrochemical parameters obtained from EIS for 45# steel in 1 M HCl solution without or with varying concentrations of CDs at 298 K.
Electrochemical parameters obtained from PDP curves for carbon steel (45#) in 1 M HCl solution without and with CDs of different concentrations at 298 K.
Electrochemical parameters for carbon steel (45#) in 1 M HCl solution with 20 ppm CDs1 at different temperatures.
Corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency were determined through PDP tests in 1 M HCl at 298–313 K, both with and without CDs1.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, functionalized carbon dots were synthesized in one step from L-histidine and imidazolyl ionic liquids using an ionic liquid-assisted microwave method as green corrosion inhibitors for steel in a 1 M HCl solution. Different ionic liquid anions (CF3COO- and HSO4 -) were introduced in the preparation of the carbon dots, and their microstructures and surface properties were characterized. Notably, the corrosion inhibition efficiency of the prepared CDs (assisted by CF3COO-) reached 94.2% at 5 mg‧dm−3. The corrosion inhibition behavior of CDs on mild steel in 1 M HCl was investigated through DFT calculations. Our investigation revealed that the anions of the selected ionic liquids have a certain influence on the structure of the carbon dots, which correlates with the performance of the carbon dot corrosion inhibitor. Therefore, this study provides guidance for the preparation of functionalized carbon dot corrosion inhibitors using a one-step microwave method and the selection of ionic liquids (as solvents, modifiers, and mediums for microwave conversion).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The utilization of metal materials is indispensable in various domains of contemporary society [1–4]. Common acidic corrosive media are widely used in industries such as oil well acidizing, chemical cleaning, processing and pickling [5]. In such cases, corrosion can lead to the renewal of various infrastructures and equipment, as well as loss of life and environmental problems, resulting in high costs [6]. The application of corrosion inhibitors has proven to be highly effective in preventing the corrosion of metallic materials [7,8]. One effective type of corrosion inhibitor for steel is chromate, but due to its high toxicity, environmental restrictions/ regulations in more countries prohibit its practical application [9,10]. Organic corrosion inhibitors are another effective corrosion inhibitor for carbon steel. According to the literature, amino acids, drug molecules [11,12], natural products and plant extracts [13–15], neutral polymers [16] and carbohydrates [17] have been developed and utilized as corrosion inhibitors. Organic corrosion inhibitors are widely used because of their sophisticated synthesis technology and good retarding effect. However, they are not readily biodegradable and can cause environmental problems [18–21].

Recently, there has been a gradual increase in public awareness regarding the significance of environmental conservation, so efficient and low-cost corrosion inhibitors have become the future direction of development, because of their myriad advantages, such as facilitating environmental preservation, cost-effectiveness, facile synthesis, extensive specific surface area, and enhanced substrate reactivity [22–24]. Carbon dots are unique nanomaterials consisting of carbon atoms bonded to neighboring carbon, oxygen, or other heteroatoms through sp2 or sp3 hybridization [25]. Due to their unique advantages, carbon dots, as a new green material, have good prospects for applications in the fields of sensing, imaging, electrochemistry and corrosion protection [22,23,26].

Carbon dots (CDs) have been reported for metal corrosion protection in recent years and are listed in Table S1. Many studies have reported that amino acids are used to synthesize carbon dots as precursors. A series of N-CDs was synthesized by high-temperature cracking at different reaction temperatures and reaction times using tryptophan as the raw material, and their corrosion retardation properties were investigated in 1 mol·L−1 HCl. Carbon dots, prepared at 180°C for one hour, exhibited the lowest corrosion current. The N-CDs formed a thick film on the steel surface by physicochemical action, thus providing protection to the steel [27]. A green corrosion inhibitor has been obtained using citric acid and L-serine as raw materials using the traditional hydrothermal method of reaction at 200°C for 18 h, which can achieve an inhibition efficiency of over 98%. The adsorption model suggests that it combines physical and chemical adsorption interactions on the copper substrate [28]. Citric acid and L-histidine, which can already be used as retardants, are used as raw materials for the reaction to synthesize a functionalized green retardant. Different concentrations of N-CDs were tested in HCl solutions at different temperatures, and their retardation efficiency was as high as 96.13% at a concentration of 200 ppm, and both were better than citric acid or L-histidine alone as retardants [29]. Recently, researchers have modified L-histidine (L-His) with 4-aminosalicylic acid (4-ASA) to synthesize a new type of highly efficient N-CDs with a size of 3–5 nm. It was found that the corrosion inhibition in an acidic environment was substantially improved compared to either 4-ASA or L-His, from below 45% initially to over 90% [30]. Therefore, carbon dots prepared from amino acids as precursors have good prospects as corrosion inhibitors, but shortening the reaction time, simplifying the preparation process, and reducing costs are still issues that should be considered. In recent years, researchers have proposed that ionic liquid-assisted solvothermal methods can effectively address the issues mentioned above. Yang et al. synthesized functionalized carbon dot corrosion inhibitors modified with (1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride using a two-step solvothermal method. Compared to carbon dots without ionic liquid modification, the corrosion inhibition performance of IL-CDs was significantly enhanced, although the role of ionic liquids in this study was not systematically investigated [31]. Li et al. synthesized CDs using the solvothermal method (180°C, 4 h), followed by the modification of carbon dots with 1-ethyl-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride. Molecular simulations and DFT calculations indicated that the superior performance of IL-CDs was attributed to a lower band gap, larger positive electrostatic potential distribution, and weaker π-π interactions, regulated by imidazolium ionic liquids. It contributes to a deeper understanding of the design and mechanism exploration of CDs as advanced corrosion inhibitors [32]. In our previous publications, L-cys as the carbon source and [Bmim]Br as an auxiliary solvent were utilized to synthesize IL-CDs via the solvothermal method. It provides a universal solvothermal approach for the synthesis of ionic liquid-assisted carbon dot inhibitors, offering guidance on the structure-inhibition relationship for the theoretical design of corrosion inhibitors [33]. These findings suggest that the technology of ionic liquid-assisted solvothermal synthesis of carbon dots in the corrosion field is gradually maturing. However, to date, no studies have addressed the impact of different ionic liquid anions on the corrosion inhibition performance of carbon dots. How should we choose the appropriate ionic liquid? Furthermore, with the increasing emphasis on environmental protection, are there more energy-efficient and environmentally friendly preparation methods that can replace solvothermal methods?

The microwave method has the advantages of saving time and energy, and environmentally friendly and has received much attention from the scientific community in recent years [34]. The use of microwave methods enables the pyrolysis of small organic molecules to be achieved in a very short period of time, resulting in the formation of carbon dots [35]. At present, some scholars have successfully prepared carbon dots by microwave to shorten the preparation time to even 3 minutes. The material has a certain retarding effect on N80 carbon steel in HCl, and the retarding efficiency can reach 84.7% at 600 ppm [36]. However, the ease of aggregation and low quantum yields of preparing carbon dots have limited their application in various domains [37]. The advantages of ionic liquids in facilitating the nucleation of carbon dots and preventing their aggregation have led to their widespread interest in the preparation and modification of carbon dots [38]. It can be seen that using ionic liquids as a carbon source, solvent, and modifier to prepare carbon dots can solve the problems associated with traditional preparation methods [38,39]. At the same time, in ionic liquid-assisted microwave preparation of carbon dots, the ionic liquid can convert the microwave into thermal energy, which is conducive to the nucleation of carbon dots [40]. Unfortunately, there is a scarcity of literature available on the influence of different anions on the shapes, compositions, and corrosion inhibition capabilities of carbon dots during microwave-assisted preparation.

In this work, how different IL anions affect the morphology, structure and anticorrosion performance of CDs were investigated. Herein, carbon dots were synthesized by ionic liquid-assisted microwave method, using L-histidine as raw material. Different IL anions, including CF3COO- and HSO4 -, have been introduced in the preparation of carbon dots (CDs). CDs exhibited excellent corrosion inhibition properties while reducing the preparation time compared to previous literature reports. Remarkably, the effectiveness of corrosion inhibitors exhibits divergences due to more nitrogen species formation in pyrrole and enhanced dispersion of carbon particles, facilitated by CF3COO- in ILs. Both quantum chemical study and molecular dynamics (MD) approach are used to interpret the inhibition mechanism of as-prepared CDs inhibitors.

L-histidine (L-His, 99%), 1-butyl-3-methylimidazole bromide ([Bmim]Br, 97%), and hexane were purchased from Aladdin. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 35%), sulfuric acid, acetone, and anhydrous ethyl ether were purchased from Tianjin Fengchuan Co., Ltd. Trifluoroacetic acid was purchased from Shanghai YI EN Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. The test electrodes were medium carbon steel (45#) with dimensions of 10*10*2 mm, and the composition of elements in steel sheets is shown in Table S2.

The preparation method of [Bmim]TFA is shown in Fig. S1(a). Following the steps, 1-Butyl-3-methylimidazole bromide salt ([Bmim]Br) and trifluoroacetic acid in a molar ratio 1:1.2 were mixed in acetone under argon gas and stirred at room temperature for 48 h. To improve purity, the slurry is then filtrated with a Gooch funnel to remove the precipitates. Subsequently, the product was washed 3 times with hexane to remove the acetone, then dried under vacuum for 12 h to get [Bmim]TFA[41].

The preparation method of [Bmim]HSO4 is shown in Fig. S1(b). Remarkably, 0.3 mol of 1-butyl-3-methylimidazole bromide salt ([Bmim]Br) and concentrated sulfuric acid were placed in a beaker, then 100 mL of anhydrous dichloromethane as the solvent was added and stirred mechanically at room temperature for 10 h to ensure the reaction completed. Purification includes filtering off the precipitate, removing the solvent by spinning and washing the ionic liquid with ether three times, and drying under a vacuum to obtain [Bmim]HSO4 [42].

The preparation process of carbon dots is shown in Fig. 1. By procedure, 1 g of 1-butyl-3-methyl trifluoroacetate or 1-butyl-3-methylimidazole dihydrogen salt and 0.55 g of L-histidine were added to a 100 mL beaker with 5 mL of water as the solvent and sonicated for 10 min to be fully dissolved. After reacting for 90 s at 800 W in a domestic microwave oven, a dark brown solution was obtained. To improve the product purity, the supernatant was taken for dialysis after centrifugation at 8000 r min−1 for 15 min. After that, the dialysis process was continued by the dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 24 h, with water changes every 6 h. Finally, the yellow solution was dried at 80°C for 4 days to obtain CDs. The products prepared with ionic liquids with different anions, CF3COO- and HSO4 -, are named CDs1 and CDs2, respectively.

The structural characterization of the carbon dots was conducted through FT-IR, PL and UV–vis spectroscopy, while XPS analysis was employed for chemical composition identification. Furthermore, TEM imaging was utilized to visualize the morphology and distribution of the carbon dots. Before the test, the various inhibitors were dispersed in ethanol by ultrasound over 6 h. Then, they were titrated on the surface of copper mesh and dried.

The efficiency evaluation of corrosion inhibitors was conducted using weight loss measurement in accordance with the standard set by NACE TM0169/G31 [38]. Prior to testing, all 10*10*2 mm steel sheets were pre-treated by mechanically polishing the surface of the sheets with #150#, #600#, #1500#, and #2000# grit wet sandpaper, and then the sheets were ultrasonically cleaned by immersing them in isopropyl alcohol, cleaned and dried with cold air from a hair dryer and weighed for initial weight. Subsequently, the steel sheets with an exposed area of 2.8 cm2 were immersed in a 1 M HCl solution without and with two corrosion inhibitors. The immersion process was carried out in a constant temperature water bath at 25°C. After the immersion was completed, the steel surface was rinsed with deionized water and then immersed in isopropyl alcohol for sonication before the surface was blown dry with cold air for weighing. The corrosion rate (mm/year) was calculated by Eq.1 [43].
(1)
Corrosion rate =
K × W
A × T × D
Where K represents a constant value, W denotes the mass loss in grams, A signifies the exposed area in square centimeters, T indicates the immersion duration in hours, and D stands for the material density measured in grams per cubic centimeter.

The conventional electrochemical workstation (Gamry, ref. 3000) was employed for the electrochemical tests. A three-electrode glass cell with an electrolyte volume of 200 mL was utilized. The working electrode consisted of medium carbon steel (45#) with an exposed area of 1 cm², while the remaining surface was coated with epoxy resin. The reference and counter electrodes were respectively an Ag/AgCl electrode filled with a saturated potassium chloride solution in fine lugging’s capillaries and a platinum electrode. Prior to each electrochemical test, the surfaces of the steel samples used as working electrodes were mechanically polished using #180#, #600#, #1500#, and #2000# grit sandpaper successively. Subsequently, they were rinsed with deionized water followed by isopropyl alcohol before being dried using cold air and wrapped in cling film for protection purposes. To initiate the electrochemical testing process, the polished electrode underwent an open circuit potential test in the test solution for one hour until system stabilization occurred. Following this step, impedance spectroscopy (EIS) testing at the open circuit potential (E ocp) took place within a frequency range spanning from 100 kHz to 50 mHz utilizing sinusoidal AC current featuring an amplitude of 10 mV. The data acquired through EIS analysis were processed using Zsimpwin software for further examination purposes. Lastly, potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) testing was conducted via EIS assessment at a scan rate set at 0.5 mV‧s−1. The Tafel extrapolation method was used to calculate corrosion potential and corrosion current density.

The steel sheet undergoes the same pretreatment as prior to conducting EIS tests. Subsequently, the steel samples are immersed in a 1 M HCl solution for a duration of 2 hours at 298 K, both in the protect and unprotect of 20 ppm CDs inhibitors. After that, distilled water serves to rinse the carbon steel surface, then dry it. Characterization of the carbon steel surface is performed utilizing scanning electron microscopy (Hitachi TM3030), atomic force microscopy (BRUKER), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher NEXSA).

Fig. 2 shows the UV–vis and FT-IR spectra of two as-prepared CDs from L-histidine combined with two different ionic liquids. The UV–vis spectra of both CDs samples display two distinct absorption bands at 240 nm and 300–400 nm, respectively. Among these, the π→π* leap of the sp2-hybridized carbon in the aromatic ring was identified as the cause of the peak at 240 nm. The absorption peaks at 275–345 can be assigned to the n→π* excitation of the CN bond and the π→π* excitation of the CO bond. The UV–vis information confirmed the successful preparation of the carbon dots [29,40,44]. In FT-IR spectra, the absorption peaks of CDs1 at 3378, 1650, 1569, 1336, and 1099 cm−1 were ascribed to -OH、N-H [29,42], N-CO [27,45], CN, C-N [46] and C-OH [46,47], respectively. Similar absorption peaks can be observed of CDs2. Notably, the presence of N-CO and CN proved the successful preparation of N-doped carbon dots. The intensity of “N-CO” for CDs1 was higher than for CDs2, while the “C–OH” intensity for CDs2 was higher than for CDs1. Differences in the surface functional groups of products caused by the two imidazolium-based ILs were different anions (TFA and HSO4 -) in the microwave-assisted preparation of carbon dots, which is related to the nature of the different ILs, the polarity of the ILs and the different radius of anions [48,49].

The XPS spectra of CDs are displayed in Fig. 3. The XPS detected the presence of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) elements in both CDs. The C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3b) contains four fitting peaks, C-C/CC (284.8 eV), C-O/C-N (286 eV), CN (287.1 eV), CO (288.2 eV) [50]. For CDs1, the O 1 s spectrum located at 531.0, 531.7, and 532.8 eV is attributed to CO, C-O-C, and OC-O bonds, respectively [51]. While the peak at 532.8 eV was non-existent of CDs2, it suggests that different ionic liquids cause differences in the functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots.

This agrees well with the UV–vis and FTIR findings, demonstrating the successful preparation of the CDs. Three peaks can be fitted to the N 1 s spectrum, located at 398.2, 399.5, and 401.1 eV, corresponding to pyridine-like, pyrrole-like, and graphite-like nitrogen, respectively [42,52]. The contents of the three nitrogen species were calculated as shown in Table S3. For comparison, the content of pyrrole nitrogen in CDs1 accounts for 55.50% of the total nitrogen, and graphite nitrogen only accounts for 14.33%. However, in CDs2, the content of pyrrole nitrogen only accounts for 16.60% of the total nitrogen, and graphite nitrogen is as high as 73.63%. The results of XPS and EDS (Fig. S2) show that ILs are involved in the reaction, and F and S are strategically introduced into CDs1 and CDs2, respectively.

The PL spectra of two CDs with a broad UV–vis absorption, when the emission spectra ranged from 360 to 420 nm, are shown in Fig. 4. Remarkably, maximum PL intensity was observed at 495.6 and 508.2 nm for CDs1 and CDs2, respectively. In addition, Fig. S3 exhibited strong blue and green luminescence under excitation at 365 nm for CDs1 and CDs2, respectively. The different colors of luminescence emitted from CDs under UV light are closely related to the different surface states because of groups (N-CO, C-OH, N-H) at the surface of CDs [51]. Compared to CDs2, the CDs1 obtained here displayed narrower PL profiles, which was related to its more uniform size distribution [39,53].

The TEM images of CDs1 and CDs2 are shown in Fig. 5. It can be seen that the two CDs were well dispersed, and none of them showed obvious agglomeration under TEM. The average particle sizes of CDs1 and CDs2 were 2.75 and 12.72 nm, respectively. Remarkably, the average particle size of CDs1 was relatively smaller, showing a normal distribution, while the average particle size of CDs2 was much larger, which can be attributed to the enhanced CDs growth in acidic ILs([Bmim]HSO4). However, the particle size dispersion of CDs2 was in a much wider range than CDs1, suggesting the degree of passivation of CDs2 differed from others because of the difference in the anions of ILs.

Fig. 6 presents Nyquist and Bode diagrams that demonstrate the behavior of a steel sheet in the blank solution, as well as solutions with varying concentrations of two corrosion inhibitors. The capacitive semicircles were observed ascribed to the charge-transfer process, which is involved in the metal corrosion reaction. It can be seen from Fig. 6a, b that a prominent inductive reactance appeared in the low-frequency region in the blank solution. This indicates that 45# steel has caused a certain degree of corrosion at the sulfuric acid environment[54]. In addition, the radius of the capacitance arc increases significantly with the addition of the corrosion inhibitor, which indicates that the CDs can form a dense film on the steel-solution surface, thus providing effective protection to the steel. In addition, both corrosion inhibitors showed an increase in the semicircular arc with their concentration, suggesting that the development of a more compact protective coating on the steel surface is aided by a rise in the concentration of corrosion inhibitor. Comparing the two corrosion inhibitors, CDs1 exhibited a larger capacitive semicircular than CDs2 at the same concentration, indicating that CDs1 has superior corrosion resistance properties at an ultra-low concentration.

For Bode plots (Fig. 6c, d), the values of impedance modulus (|Z|) at the lowest frequency had a positive relation with CDs corrosion inhibitor concentration. Moreover, the inhibition efficiency decreased in the order of CDs1 ＞ CDs2, under the CDs inhibitor concentration of 20 ppm. Significantly, the |Z| exhibited an upward inclination with increasing concentration, while the frequency range with the maximum phase angle increased as well, showing the same trend. This result suggests that increasing the concentration of carbon dots enhances the corrosion inhibition effect.

Figure S4 presents the equivalent circuit diagram model, according to the EIS result with and without CDs inhibition. By calculation, the resulting parameters are listed in Table 1. The solution resistance and charge transfer resistance are represented symbolically by R s and R ct, respectively. The CPE is the element of constant phase angle used to represent the capacitive properties of the bilayer [55], which can be calculated by the following Eq.2 [56].

Table 1 reveals the increase in R ct, compared to unprotected steel, is attributed to the constitution of CDs adsorption layer at the steel/solution interface. An increase in inhibitor concentration leads to a corresponding increase in the thickness of the electric double-layer, accompanied by a reduction in both the exposed area of the electrode and the permittivity of the film. In addition, the R ct values demonstrated an increasing trend with the rise in CDs concentration. The reason The results reveal that a denser adsorption film is formed on the surface as the concentration of carbon point corrosion inhibitor increases [58]. Table 1 summarizes the fitted data from EIS analysis. A significant increase in R ct, while the obvious decrease in C dl value can be obtained after the addition of a corrosion inhibitor. This can be attributed to the fact that the inhibitors can replace the pre-adsorbed water on the metal surface, forming a protective layer. At an ultra-low concentration of 20 ppm, the corrosion inhibitor efficiencies decreased as follows: CDs1(97.0%) > CDs2(61.3%).

The impact of the corrosion inhibitor on the corrosion rate of carbon steel in a 1 M HCl solution was evaluated through potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) curves, as illustrated in Fig. 7. The following parameters were calculated by the Tafel extrapolation method using Tafel curves: corrosion potential (E corr), corrosion current density (i corr), anodic Tafel slope (β a), cathodic Tafel slope (β c) and corrosion inhibition efficiency (η), as shown in Table 2, where η was calculated by following Eq.5 [58].

corr and i corr represented the current densities of the corrosion uninhibited and inhibited steel, respectively.

Fig. 7 and Table 2 illustrate that the i corr has a decreasing trend following the presence of the two corrosion inhibitors relative to the blank solution, which indicates that the addition of corrosion inhibitors has a certain inhibition effect on corrosion. There were no clear changes in the slope of PDP curves, which means that the presence the inhibitors does not change the corrosion mechanism against acid attacks [33]. For different corrosion inhibitor solutions, the corrosion current decreased, and the inhibitory effect of corrosion inhibitors increased significantly with CDs concentration. At an ultra-low concentration of 20 ppm, the inhibition efficiency of CDs1 was as high as 97.2%, while the inhibition efficiency of CDs2 was only 61.9%, which agrees well with the EIS results.

The EIS image of the corrosion inhibition effect of 20 ppm CDs1 in 1 M HCl for carbon steel with temperature variation was shown in Fig. 8. The EIS data was analyzed through the equivalent circuit, depicted in Fig. S3, and the electrochemical data are listed in Table 3. It indicates that the radius of the capacitance arc decreases significantly as the temperature increases from 298 K to 313 K. Meanwhile, the η of CDs1 decreases from 97.0% to 79.2% due to the increase in temperature, causing the desorption of CDs1 adsorbed on the surface of the carbon steel, which causes a decrease in the performance of the corrosion inhibitor. Remarkably, the η of CDs1 can remain to reach more than 90% at 313 K.

The weight loss measurement is a powerful tool to evaluate the inhibition performance. To investigate the impact of different CDs inhibitors, the inhibition performance was evaluated through weight loss analysis, and the consequences are presented in Fig. 9. The corrosion rate, assessed in 1 M HCl solution, was reduced after the addition of diverse CDs inhibitors among which corrosion inhibitor CDs1 decreased more significantly. The corrosion rate was only 1.67 mm/year at 24 h immersion in CDs1 solution, which is lower than CDs2. In comparison, the corrosion rate of the blank solution was as high as 45.26 mm/year, which indicates that CDs1 has a better corrosion inhibition effect. In addition, the corrosion rate of the steel sheet in the hydrochloric acid solution, with the addition of corrosion inhibitor CDs1 showed a more significant downward trend with increasing immersion time. This result confirms that CDs inhibitors further showed excellent corrosion resistance.

SEM images, as shown in Fig. 10 were taken after the steel sheets were soaked in 1 M HCl solution with or without 20 ppm of the as-prepared CDs1 and CDs2 for 2 h. It is distinct that the very smooth inhibited surface of the steel with only a few scratches, suggesting almost no corrosion by the hydrochloric acid was observed. However, we observed a significant quantity of corrosion products, and the immersion of the steel sheet in the hydrochloric acid created a very rough and heavily cracked surface. The addition of CDs inhibitors resulted in a significantly smoother surface of the steel than when the inhibitor was not added, while the surface of the steel with CDs1 was smoother than the surface of the steel with CDs2, indicating that inhibitors can effectively inhibit corrosion of carbon steel against hydrochloric acid attack and that CDs1 had better corrosion inhibition effect. The SEM result is aligning with the outcomes of the EIS analysis.

The results of the AFM tests are illustrated in Fig. 11. The surface roughness of the carbon steel (45#) surface was 236 nm without a corrosion inhibitor. However, roughness decreased to 70.9 and 189 nm in the presence of CDs1 and CDs2, respectively. The CDs inhibitor played a role in inhibiting the corrosion of carbon steel (45#) which occurred in the 1 M HCl solution, as a result, the lowest roughness was found after the addition of CDs1, which indicates that CDs1 had excellent corrosion inhibition performance. These AFM results are consistent with the weight loss and EIS analysis.

The XPS analysis for the steel surface following 2 h in the acidic solution of CDs1 is shown in Fig. 12. Three peaks that correspond to the various properties of C can be seen in the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum, such peak information as 284.5 eV, 285.8 eV, and 288.1 eV are attributed to graphitic structure C-C/CC, C–N bonds and CO bonds, respectively, as listed in Fig. 12(a) [60,61]. Fig. 12(b) shows the N 1 s peak, which can be divided into peaks at 398.8, 400.8, and 401.4 eV. The peak at 400.8 eV was attributed to the coordinated nitrogen (N) in the CDs1. The occurrence of the third peak was attributed to the protonation (N+H) of nitrogen in the CDs inhibitor [62]. This means that the adsorption type of inhibitor in the steel/solution interface belonged to the mixed adsorption model [63]. Among them, C-N is fitted with the first peak in the inhibitor [64]. In the O 1 s spectrum in Fig. 12(c), there are three signals at 529.6, 531.0, and 532.8 eV, as shown in O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 12c). The first was ascribed to the O2−, and the latter two were assigned to -OH which comes from hydrous iron oxides (such as FeOOH) [42].

The Langmuir adsorption model fitted the curve, and the adsorption isotherm was attained using Eq.6 [65].

The values of R2 of CDs1 and CDs2 are 0.9999 and 0.9905, respectively; the correlation between C and C/θ is demonstrated in Fig. 13 that the adsorption process of both corrosion inhibitors in the corrosion process conforms to the Langmuir adsorption. K ads can be used to evaluate the adsorption strength [67]. In general, there is a positive correlation between the value of K ads and the intensity of adsorption. The value K ads in descending order are CDs1 > CDs2, which indicates that CDs1 has a more robust adsorption capacity at the steel/solution interface and, therefore, has better corrosion inhibition performance. Table 4 shows the corrosion rates for carbon steel (45# steel) obtained from EIS analysis at different temperatures uninhibited or inhibited by different concentrations of CDs in 1 M HCl solution. The corrosion rate decreases significantly with the addition of corrosion inhibitors at the same temperature, while the efficiency of corrosion inhibition shows a decreasing trend as the concentration of corrosion inhibitors increases. With a rise in temperature, the corrosion rate escalates while the efficiency of corrosion inhibition diminishes under the premise of the same corrosion inhibitor concentration.

The kinetic parameters were obtained from the electrochemical data from 298 to 313 K, and then the Arrhenius diagram was plotted according to the following Eq.8 [69].
(8)

where the corrosion rate, denoted as C

The prefactor is represented as P, while R, a default value of 8.314 (J‧mol−1), corresponds to the gas constant. The T represents the thermodynamic Kelvin temperature.

The determination of activation enthalpy (ΔH a) and activation entropy (ΔS a) involves individual calculations based on the analysis of the transition state diagram. These values can be obtained by combining the slope and intercept derived from this diagram using a specific equation.

ΔH a and ΔS a were calculated using Eq.9, and the slope and intercept of the straight line were obtained in the transition curve diagram (Fig. 14b), respectively [70].

Where the Planck constant h and Avogadro's number N, were employed in the computation of activation enthalpy and activation entropy. The corresponding values calculated at different concentrations can be found in Table 5. The rise in concentration resulted in larger values of ΔH a and ΔS a. All ΔH a were positive, indicating that the corrosion process of carbon steel in HCl solution with the presence of corrosion inhibitor is a heat absorption process, with a gradual increase in ΔS a in the adsorption process with increasing concentration, on account of the activation complexes were formed on the steel surface [71].

A quantum chemical analysis was conducted to investigate the structural correlation of organic corrosion inhibitors, aiming to elucidate their mechanism of action. The structures of the molecules are derived from XPS and FT-IR. The optimized configurations and frontier molecular orbitals (FMO) are depicted in Fig. 15. Obviously, the HOMO and LUMO spread on the whole molecular skeleton of CDs1. However, the LUMO and HUMO of CDs2 are located at the partial molecular skeleton. Therefore, the comprehensive coverage of the inhibitor molecule by CDs1's FMO distribution suggests that the inhibitor has the potential for adsorbing on the steel surface in a flat or quasi-flat manner, indicating a robust interaction between CDs inhibitors and metal interface. It is widely recognized that the E LUMO value reflects the electron-accepting capability of organic molecules, with a lower value indicating stronger electron acceptance ability. Moreover, a higher EHOMO value signifies an enhanced electron supply capacity. [56,72].

The interaction energy between the CDs inhibitor and the Fe surface was calculated using molecular simulation techniques. The simulation box size is 37.24 Å× 37.24 Å ×56.31 Å. The first layer is the Fe slab with five sub-layer of Fe (1 1 0) atoms, and the second layer contains 1 molecule of corrosion inhibitor, 480 molecules of water, 10 molecules of H+, and 10 molecules of Cl-. A vacuum layer of 30 Å was added above the second layer. The Forcite package completed the MD simulation. The simulation system is simulated for 5000 ps using COMPASSⅡ force field under the constant volume constant internal energy (NVT ensemble) with an integration time step of 1 fs at 298 K. The interaction energy (E interact) between CDs inhibitors and Fe substrate and binding energy E binding are calculated as follows.
(10)
E interact=E total-(E subs+E inhibitors)
(11)
E
binding =- E
interact
Where E tot represents the total energy of the whole system, E subs is the energy of Fe substrate and E inh is free inhibitor energy.

Fig. 16 depicts the equilibrium adsorption configurations of CDs inhibitors by both side and top perspectives. As shown in Fig. 16, the CDs inhibitors can be adsorbed on the Fe surface with parallel mode due to its CDs structure containing functional groups and conjugated network. This functional group and conjugated system with CDs inhibitors are conducive to forming the absorption film on the Fe surface. The simulation results can be explained by electron donation and electrostatic occurring between the Fe surface and CDs inhibitors. At the interface between CDs inhibitors and Fe surfaces, the CDs inhibitors can denote lone electron pairs to unfilled 3d orbital of Fe; meanwhile, the electrons can be back donated from Fe atom to the conjugated system of CDs inhibitors (antibonding). This parallel adsorption pattern contributes to minimizing the exposure area with Fe surface in a corrosive solution, thus reducing corrosion attack.

The E binding between the corresponding CDs inhibitors molecules and the Fe (1 1 0) substance was calculated to be 374.94 kJ·mol−1 (CDs1) and 349.01 kJ·mol−1 (CDs2). The CDs1 exhibits higher E binding values, which further demonstrated that its higher corrosion inhibition efficiency η for carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution.

The corrosion inhibition mechanism of the corrosion inhibitors was shown in Fig. 17. The N species in the corrosion inhibitor at which the presence of the three forms of nitrogen is related to the corrosion inhibition effect. Pyrrole and Pyridine nitrogen in CDs are attributed to chemisorption and, therefore, facilitate the establishment of coordination bonds between the heteroatoms and the steel surface, while the presence of graphite nitrogen allows for an agglomeration effect on the surface, resulting in a stormy steel surface that is attacked by Fe2+ and Cl- [73]. CDs1 has a higher content of pyrrole and pyridine nitrogen and the lowest content of graphitic nitrogen, making it a more chemisorbed hybrid adsorption model with well-dispersed material that is less prone to surface agglomeration and can form a dense film on the steel surface. However, CDs2 has a higher graphitic nitrogen content, resulting in heavy aggregation on its surface, which is not conducive to the formation of a protective film.

One-step microwave method has been used to synthesize functionalized carbon dots from L-histidine in the presence of ionic liquids. The anions of ionic liquids, including CF3COO- and HSO4 -, have a particular impact on the structure and morphology of carbon dots, thereby influencing the performance of carbon dot corrosion inhibitors. The following conclusions were obtained:
(1)
The XPS and EDS results show that both F from CF3COO- and S element from HSO4 - in ILs were successfully doped into the carbon dots, suggesting that the anion of ionic liquid was also involved in the CDs formation. Besides, the XPS shows that CDs1, from CF3COO- based IL, has a higher content of pyrrole N and less graphitic nitrogen than CDs2.
(2)
The HRTEM shows that the particle sizes of CDs1 and CDs2 were 2.75 and 12.72 nm, respectively, which might be explained by the fact that the acidity of HSO4 - (CDs2) facilitates the growth of CDs, resulting in bigger CDs2.
(3)
The Tafel and EIS results showed that CDs1 (from CF3COO- based ILs) had better corrosion inhibition performance (5 ppm, 94.2%), attributed to the formation of more pyrrole-like nitrogen species and better homogeneity.
(4)
Both quantum chemical study and molecular dynamics approaches are used to study the inhibition mechanism of the CD inhibitors. The high E binding value of CDs1 displayed stronger adsorption on the steel surface, leading to better inhibiting performance at a molecular or atomic level.
(5)
This study provides a pathway for designing functionalized carbon dots by a one-step microwave synthesis method in the presence of ionic liquids, working as solvents, modifiers, and microwave conversion media.
